players.name: Manuel Neuer
players.bio: The sweeper-keeper who redefined goalkeeping. Neuer, 124 caps and counting, is a World Cup winner (2014) and multiple Bundesliga champion. At 40, he remains Germany's last line — and first outfield player.
players.facts: Neuer once dribbled the ball so far from his goal in a World Cup match that pundits debated whether he was a goalkeeper or a libero.

players.name: Antonio Rüdiger
players.bio: Berlin-born bruiser who plays for Real Madrid with the intensity of a man who has something to prove at all times. Rüdiger is physically dominant, fearless, and occasionally terrifying — for opponents and teammates alike.
players.facts: Rüdiger is known for his pre-match ritual of biting his own hand. Opponents consider themselves lucky that's the only thing he bites.

players.name: Waldemar Anton
players.bio: A composed and versatile defender at Borussia Dortmund, Anton has quietly built an international career on reliability rather than headlines. Still adding caps, he's a solid presence in Germany's defensive ranks.
players.facts: Born in Hannover to parents of Russian-German heritage, Anton was a late bloomer who didn't earn his first senior cap until his mid-twenties.

players.name: Jonathan Tah
players.bio: The towering Hamburger-born centre-back joined Bayern Munich after years of Bayer Leverkusen loyalty. Tah is an aerial powerhouse and composed ball-player whose 47 caps reflect steady trust from the national setup.
players.facts: Tah was part of Germany's squad at Euro 2024 on home soil and is of Sierra Leonean heritage — a proud dual-identity he's spoken about openly.

players.name: Aleksandar Pavlović
players.bio: A teenager-turned-international, the Bayern Munich midfielder announced himself with a stunning debut goal for Germany. Pavlović is technically gifted with an old head on young shoulders — and 2026 could be his coming-out party.
players.facts: Born in 2004, Pavlović is young enough that he was still in primary school when Germany won the 2014 World Cup.

players.name: Joshua Kimmich
players.bio: Germany's metronome with 110 caps, Kimmich can play right-back or central midfield — and excels at both with irritating ease. A serial winner at Bayern Munich and one of Europe's most complete footballers of his generation.
players.facts: Kimmich controversially delayed his COVID-19 vaccination in 2021, sparking a national debate in Germany. He later got vaccinated after contracting the virus.

players.name: Kai Havertz
players.bio: The Aachen-born forward scored the 2021 Champions League winning goal for Chelsea and has since thrived at Arsenal. Elegant, intelligent, and versatile, Havertz at 27 is entering his peak years just in time for 2026.
players.facts: Havertz scored the winning penalty in the 2021 Champions League final — coolly chipping the ball past the keeper like it was a training drill.

players.name: Leon Goretzka
players.bio: A box-to-box dynamo from Bochum who combines physical power with technical quality. Goretzka has been a key figure at Bayern and Germany for years, netting 15 international goals — not bad for a midfielder.
players.facts: Goretzka is a vocal advocate for social causes and publicly confronted a far-right political symbol during Euro 2020 in a widely shared moment.

players.name: Jamie Leweling
players.bio: The Stuttgart winger with pace to burn, Leweling is one of Germany's exciting new faces. Quick, direct, and with a nose for goal, the Nürnberg-born flyer is making a strong case for a starring role at 2026.
players.facts: Leweling's father is American, making him one of several Germany players with dual heritage — fitting for a World Cup partly hosted by the USA.

players.name: Jamal Musiala
players.bio: Born in Stuttgart, raised partly in England, now the jewel of German football. Musiala's dribbling through tight spaces borders on the supernatural. At just 23, he carries the #10 and the nation's hopes with a smile.
players.facts: Musiala chose Germany over England despite qualifying for both — a decision Three Lions fans are still quietly devastated about.

players.name: Nick Woltemade
players.bio: The Bremen-born striker made a rapid rise from Stuttgart to Newcastle United, catching the eye with 4 goals in just 11 caps. Woltemade is tall, technically assured, and hungry — a classic No.9 in the making.
players.facts: Woltemade shares his Valentine's Day birthday (14 Feb) with a select club of footballers — arguably the most romantic striker in the squad.

players.name: Oliver Baumann
players.bio: The Hoffenheim stalwart has waited patiently behind Neuer for much of his career and finally earned his international opportunity. Baumann is a reliable, experienced shot-stopper with Bundesliga class in abundance.
players.facts: Baumann spent over a decade at Hoffenheim, making him one of the most loyal one-club men in the Bundesliga — a rarity in modern football.

players.name: Pascal Groß
players.bio: Proof that it's never too late: the Mannheim-born midfielder didn't earn his first Germany cap until his 30s. Groß has been a creative force at Brighton and brings intelligence and set-piece quality to the squad.
players.facts: Groß made his Germany debut at 32 years old — older than some international players' entire careers. Better late than never, Pascal.

players.name: Maximilian Beier
players.bio: The Brandenburg-born striker joined Borussia Dortmund and has impressed with his movement and finishing instincts. Still developing at 23, Beier is one for the future — though the future might just be 2026.
players.facts: Beier was born in Brandenburg an der Havel, a city most famous for its medieval cathedral. He's adding some modern-day goals to its legacy.

players.name: Nico Schlotterbeck
players.bio: Younger brother of Keven Schlotterbeck, Nico has forged his own path at Dortmund with commanding centre-back displays. Quick, left-footed, and composed, the Waiblingen-born defender is a modern defensive cornerstone.
players.facts: Both Nico and his older brother Keven Schlotterbeck are professional footballers — a rare sibling pairing both operating at the top level of German football.

players.name: Angelo Stiller
players.bio: The Stuttgart midfielder is quietly becoming one of the Bundesliga's finest deep-lying playmakers. Stiller reads the game brilliantly and distributes with composure — a player whose influence rarely shows in the stats.
players.facts: Stiller is a graduate of Bayern Munich's famed academy — yet it was at Stuttgart where he truly found his voice as a footballer.

players.name: Florian Wirtz
players.bio: Germany's golden boy. Wirtz made his Bundesliga debut at 17, suffered a serious ACL injury, and came back better than ever. Now at Liverpool, the 23-year-old is arguably the most exciting player in world football.
players.facts: Wirtz scored his first Bundesliga goal at 17 years and 34 days old — making him the youngest scorer in Bundesliga history at the time.

players.name: Nathaniel Brown
players.bio: The young Eintracht Frankfurt defender is one of Germany's most exciting defensive prospects. Brown brings athleticism and composure beyond his years, and 2026 could mark his arrival on the world stage.
players.facts: Brown holds dual American-German nationality — making him perfectly placed for a World Cup co-hosted on American soil.

players.name: Leroy Sané
players.bio: Electric pace, sublime skill, and maddening inconsistency — Sané has always been Germany's great enigma. Now at Galatasaray, the 30-year-old arrives at 2026 with a point to prove and the ability to light up any stadium.
players.facts: Sané was controversially left out of Germany's 2018 World Cup squad by Löw despite a brilliant season at Man City. Germany went out in the group stage.

players.name: Nadiem Amiri
players.bio: The Mainz creative midfielder of Afghan heritage brings flair and unpredictability in equal measure. Amiri has the ability to unlock defences with a single pass or dribble, and 2026 is a chance to shine on football's biggest stage.
players.facts: Amiri's family fled Afghanistan before he was born — his journey from Heidelberg to the World Cup is one of football's most compelling backstories.

players.name: Alexander Nübel
players.bio: The Paderborn-born goalkeeper has long been seen as the heir apparent to Neuer. After loan spells honing his craft, Nübel is now Stuttgart's undisputed No.1 and ready to step up for Germany if called upon.
players.facts: Nübel made the controversial move to become Neuer's backup at Bayern Munich — essentially paying to sit on the bench for two years.

players.name: David Raum
players.bio: The marauding left-back from Nürnberg is one of Europe's most attack-minded defenders. Raum loves to overlap, delivers dangerous crosses, and has become a key creative outlet for both RB Leipzig and Germany.
players.facts: Raum registered more assists than many wingers during his standout Bundesliga seasons — proof that full-backs have completely taken over football.

players.name: Felix Nmecha
players.bio: Born in Hamburg, Felix Nmecha is the younger brother of Lukas Nmecha and a powerful central midfielder at Dortmund. He combines physicality with technical quality, marking himself as one of Germany's rising midfield options.
players.facts: Felix and Lukas Nmecha are brothers who have both represented Germany — a feat that puts them in very select international sibling company.

players.name: Malick Thiaw
players.bio: The Düsseldorf-born centre-back joined Newcastle United after impressing at AC Milan. Thiaw is tall, composed, and surprisingly comfortable on the ball — a modern defender built for high defensive lines.
players.facts: Thiaw is of Senegalese heritage and could have represented Senegal internationally — Germany moved quickly to secure his allegiance. Smart business.

players.name: Assan Ouédraogo
players.bio: Born in 2006, Ouédraogo is the squad's youngest player and already has an international goal to his name. The RB Leipzig teenager is frighteningly good for his age and could be Germany's midfield future for a decade.
players.facts: Ouédraogo scored on his Germany debut — at just 18 years old. He was literally born after Germany won Euro 1996. Let that sink in.

players.name: Deniz Undav
players.bio: The Varel-born striker took the scenic route to the top — non-league football and Belgian second division included. Undav's remarkable rise to 6 international goals in 9 caps is the feel-good story of the German squad.
players.facts: Undav played in Belgium's lower leagues before his career took off. He went from obscurity to a World Cup squad in just a few astonishing years.
